prompt: A city increases CO2 reduction policies. Which response best describes the system relationship for emissions to impacts?
options: Policies will directly reduce emissions and impacts with no intermediate links
Policies reduce emissions, which can increase temperature effects only if feedbacks are involved
Policies reduce emissions, strengthening the greenhouse mechanism less, which can change climate impacts
Policies reduce emissions and, through greenhouse effect changes, can shift temperature and downstream impacts, including possible feedbacks and delays

prompt: Which option best identifies a feedback loop in a warming system?
options: Warming increases CO2, which increases warming by direct injection with no loop-back mechanism
Warming reduces ice, lowering reflectivity (albedo), causing more warming that further reduces ice
Warming increases rainfall and this always decreases CO2 immediately
Warming and ice loss are unrelated

prompt: Suppose a country starts mitigation today. Why might the strongest impacts not appear immediately?
options: Because the system resets each month, so impacts are random
Because there are delays (e.g., oceans/ice respond over time), so outcomes lag behind actions
Because feedbacks stop once mitigation starts
Because CO2 instantly returns to zero

prompt: A policy boosts biofuels to cut emissions. Which response best reasons about unintended consequences?
options: Only emissions matter; land and ecosystems never change outcomes
Land-use changes can affect food/ecosystems, creating indirect impacts that interact with climate risks
Biofuels always reduce risks with no trade-offs
Unintended consequences cannot be predicted

prompt: Which response best captures system interactions in sustainability decisions?
options: Energy decisions affect cost only; they do not affect other resources
Energy and water can influence each other through usage and reliability, so changing one part can cascade
Water influences weather only; energy choices never matter
Systems thinking means using more numbers, not relationships
